data.title: Hard Bop & First Great Quintet
data.article:
The First Great Quintet — Miles Davis, John Coltrane, Red Garland, Paul Chambers, Philly Joe Jones — came together in 1955 and immediately set a new standard for small-group jazz.

The Prestige sessions of 1956, recorded in marathon one-day dates to fulfil a contract, produced four albums of ferocious, hard-swinging music: *Cookin'*, *Relaxin'*, *Workin'*, and *Steamin'*. Trane's sheets of sound against Miles's economical lyricism created a productive tension that drove both men to new heights.

Meanwhile, Miles signed with Columbia and began collaborating with arranger Gil Evans on a series of large-ensemble recordings — *Miles Ahead* (1957), *Porgy and Bess* (1958) — that expanded his orchestral ambitions.

It culminated in April 1959, when Miles walked into Columbia's 30th Street Studio with a new band — Coltrane, Cannonball Adderley, Bill Evans, Paul Chambers, Jimmy Cobb — and recorded *Kind of Blue* in essentially one take per track.
